title: Do Shared Decoders Improve Prototype-Edit Reusability on Frozen CLIP Features?
description: Run a benchmark-style empirical study of whether sharing one decoder across activation reconstruction and paired-difference reconstruction improves reusable prototype edits in frozen CLIP vision features. Compare vanilla SAE, SSAE, and a shared-decoder anchor-plus-shift model under compute-matched training, shared edit-scale normalization, and uncertainty-aware evaluation.
motivation: Recent CLIP/VLM sparse-autoencoder work already covers concept discovery, steering, and shared sparse spaces, so the novelty here can only be a narrow empirical question. The unresolved gap is whether a shared decoder gives a practical benefit over SSAE for prototype-edit reuse on the same frozen CLIP features, rather than merely reconstructing individual examples or single paired differences.
proposed_approach: Use frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 pooled pre-projection vision features as the sole training representation and compare three compute-matched models: vanilla SAE, SSAE, and a shared-decoder anchor-plus-shift model with nonnegative anchor codes, signed sparse shift codes, and a stop-gradient tie loss. Evaluate on 3D Shapes and a reduced non-sensitive CelebA subset using held-out prototype edits, shared edit-scale normalization, DINO-based pair mining, and an auxiliary CLIP-to-DINO transfer evaluation so conclusions are not drawn only inside CLIP geometry.

related_work.title: Sparse Shift Autoencoders for Identifying Concepts from Large Language Model Activations
related_work.authors: Shruti Joshi, Andrea Dittadi, Sebastien Lachapelle, Dhanya Sridhar
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the main direct baseline and novelty threat. The proposed work does not try to replace SSAE; it asks only whether sharing the decoder with an activation-reconstruction path improves prototype-edit reuse on frozen CLIP features under matched compute.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Learn Monosemantic Features in Vision-Language Models
related_work.authors: Mateusz Pach, Shyamgopal Karthik, Quentin Bouniot, Serge Belongie, Zeynep Akata
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper already establishes strong nearby precedent for interpretable sparse features and interventions in VLMs. The proposal differs only by benchmarking activation-only, shift-only, and shared-decoder training for reusable prototype edits.

related_work.title: Interpreting CLIP with Hierarchical Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.authors: Vladimir Zaigrajew, Hubert Baniecki, Przemyslaw Biecek
related_work.how_it_differs: MSAE already studies sparse concept discovery and manipulation in CLIP pooled features. The proposed work therefore cannot claim novelty from using SAEs on CLIP; it focuses on a narrower compute-matched benchmark against SSAE.

related_work.title: Steering CLIP's vision transformer with sparse autoencoders
related_work.authors: Sonia Joseph, Praneet Suresh, Ethan Goldfarb, Lorenz Hufe, Yossi Gandelsman, Robert Graham, Danilo Bzdok, Wojciech Samek, Blake Aaron Richards
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides direct evidence that CLIP sparse features can support interventions. The proposed work differs by testing prototype reuse on pooled features and by making SSAE the main comparator.

related_work.title: Visual Sparse Steering: Improving Zero-shot Image Classification with Sparsity Guided Steering Vectors
related_work.authors: Gerasimos Chatzoudis, Zhuowei Li, Gemma E. Moran, Hao Wang, Dimitris N. Metaxas
related_work.how_it_differs: Visual Sparse Steering and PASS already cover sparse steering for downstream zero-shot accuracy. The proposal does not optimize classification; it evaluates edit locality and transferability of one reusable prototype vector.

related_work.title: SPARC: Concept-Aligned Sparse Autoencoders for Cross-Model and Cross-Modal Interpretability
related_work.authors: Ali Nasiri-Sarvi, Hassan Rivaz, Mahdi S. Hosseini
related_work.how_it_differs: SPARC already learns shared sparse spaces across models and modalities. The proposal is far narrower: one frozen CLIP vision feature space, no multimodal alignment claim, and no broad shared-latent contribution.

related_work.title: LUCID-SAE: Learning Unified Vision-Language Sparse Codes for Interpretable Concept Discovery
related_work.authors: Difei Gu, Yunhe Gao, Gerasimos Chatzoudis, Zihan Dong, Guoning Zhang, Bangwei Guo, Yang Zhou, Mu Zhou, Dimitris Metaxas
related_work.how_it_differs: LUCID-SAE is a strong 2026 novelty threat for unified sparse vision-language codes. The proposal avoids that territory and limits itself to a single-space CLIP benchmark on prototype-edit reuse.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis
related_work.authors: Patrick Leask, Bart Bussmann, Michael Pearce, Joseph Bloom, Curt Tigges, Noura Al Moubayed, Lee Sharkey, Neel Nanda
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper motivates the proposal's restrained framing. The contribution is not canonical concept recovery but empirical comparison of edit locality and reusability.

related_work.title: How Reliable are Causal Probing Interventions?
related_work.authors: Marc Canby, Adam Davies, Chirag Rastogi, Julia Hockenmaier
related_work.how_it_differs: Supplies the target-change versus non-target-preservation framing. The proposed work adapts that reliability lens to frozen representation edits rather than full causal interventions.

hypothesis: On the same frozen CLIP pooled vision features and under compute-matched training, a shared decoder trained jointly on activation reconstruction and paired differences improves held-out prototype-edit reusability over vanilla SAE and yields a measurable advantage over SSAE that persists after shared edit-scale normalization.
success_criteria: On CelebA, the mean ASD-minus-SSAE prototype-reliability gap is positive, the hierarchical 95 percent bootstrap confidence interval excludes zero, and the direction matches in at least two of three seeds.

On 3D Shapes, ASD improves exact-factor locality over both baselines by at least 5 absolute points, with paired bootstrap 95 percent confidence intervals excluding zero for at least two retained factors.
The ranking is stable across at least two edit-strength values, so gains are not explained only by raw edit magnitude.
An auxiliary CLIP-to-DINO transfer evaluation shows the same effect direction on at least one dataset, indicating the result is not purely an artifact of scoring inside CLIP geometry.
